title: Traditional Latin Mass Companion
subtitle: Trust-first, offline companion for the 1962 Mass with bundled 2026 Sunday and major-feast coverage for Low and Sung Mass use, rooted in public-domain hand missal tradition and explicit about its limits.

coverageWindow.title: 2026 Bundled Sunday and Feast Coverage
coverageWindow.description: The app bundles Sundays and major feasts for calendar year 2026. Weekdays and uncovered dates still fall back to the fixed Ordinary of the Mass without inventing missing propers, silently filling gaps, or pretending to cover more than the app actually ships.

sources.title: Missale Romanum (1962), Ordinary of the Mass
sources.description: The fixed Order of Mass used as the baseline structure for every resolved day in the app.
sources.note: Used as the structural source for the Ordinary and for phase-based guide orientation throughout the app.
sources.attribution: Ordinary baseline reviewed against the 1962 Roman Missal and public-domain hand missals, especially the Fr. Lasance missal tradition.
sources.coverageNote: Applies to every date, including dates outside the bundled coverage window.

sources.title: Public-domain English hand missal translations
sources.description: English renderings and explanatory notes adapted from public-domain missal material.
sources.note: Used for side-by-side devotional reading, newcomer guidance, and explanatory copy that stays close to hand missal language without claiming to be a full edition.
sources.rights: Public-domain or adapted public-domain devotional language.
sources.attribution: Compiled from late nineteenth- and early twentieth-century English hand missal material, with Fr. Lasance as the primary editorial anchor.
sources.coverageNote: Supports the Ordinary, learning material, and explanatory notes.

sources.title: Gregorian chant primers and public-domain chant reference material
sources.description: Introductory chant guidance adapted from public-domain liturgical handbooks and chant primers.
sources.note: Used for chant learning content and for the sung landmarks surfaced in the Sung Mass profile; kept text-only for 1.0 clarity.
sources.rights: Public-domain chant reference and editorially adapted explanatory language.
sources.attribution: Compiled from public-domain chant primers and traditional liturgical reference material.
sources.coverageNote: Supports the text-only chant section and Sung Mass profile guidance.

sources.title: Traditional Roman calendar reference for 2026
sources.description: Date mapping used to bundle Sundays and major feasts for the supported 2026 coverage window.
sources.note: Used to map exact dates to celebration records without online computation, hidden rules engines, or guessed runtime substitutions.
sources.rights: Calendar facts and feast ordering used as reference metadata.
sources.attribution: Traditional Roman calendar structure reviewed against 1962-era references for 2026 scheduling.
sources.coverageNote: Applies only to the bundled 2026 coverage window.

sources.title: Seasonal proper set: Advent
sources.description: Bundled Advent Sunday proper summaries and representative prayer text for the supported year.
sources.note: Used for Advent Sundays in the 2026 bundle.
sources.rights: Bundled from public-domain seasonal missal material and editorial adaptation.
sources.attribution: Editorially adapted from public-domain traditional Advent proper sources.
sources.coverageNote: Referenced by Advent Sundays and the Immaculate Conception.

sources.title: Seasonal proper set: Christmastide and Epiphany
sources.description: Bundled Christmas and early-January Sunday proper summaries for the supported year.
sources.note: Used for Christmas, Epiphany, and Christmastide Sundays in the 2026 bundle.
sources.rights: Bundled from public-domain seasonal missal material and editorial adaptation.
sources.attribution: Editorially adapted from public-domain Christmas and Epiphany proper sources.
sources.coverageNote: Referenced by Christmastide Sundays, Epiphany, and Christmas Day.

sources.title: Seasonal proper set: Pre-Lent and Lent
sources.description: Bundled proper summaries for Septuagesima through Holy Week in the supported year.
sources.note: Used for pre-Lent Sundays, Lent, Passiontide, Palm Sunday, Saint Joseph, and the Annunciation.
sources.rights: Bundled from public-domain seasonal missal material and editorial adaptation.
sources.attribution: Editorially adapted from public-domain traditional Lent and Holy Week proper sources.
sources.coverageNote: Referenced across the pre-Lent and Lent cycle in 2026.

sources.title: Seasonal proper set: Eastertide and Ascension
sources.description: Bundled proper summaries for Easter Sunday through Pentecost in the supported year.
sources.note: Used for Eastertide Sundays, Ascension, and Pentecost.
sources.rights: Bundled from public-domain seasonal missal material and editorial adaptation.
sources.attribution: Editorially adapted from public-domain Eastertide proper sources.
sources.coverageNote: Referenced by the Paschal cycle in 2026.

sources.title: Seasonal proper set: Sundays after Pentecost
sources.description: Bundled proper summaries for the long post-Pentecost cycle in the supported year.
sources.note: Used for Trinity season and later Sundays after Pentecost.
sources.rights: Bundled from public-domain seasonal missal material and editorial adaptation.
sources.attribution: Editorially adapted from public-domain post-Pentecost proper sources.
sources.coverageNote: Referenced by post-Pentecost Sundays, Corpus Christi, Sacred Heart, Assumption, Christ the King, and All Saints.

parts.title: Prayers at the Foot of the Altar
parts.summary: Mass begins with prayers of humility and purification before the priest ascends the altar.

gestureCues.label: Stand or kneel according to local custom
gestureCues.detail: Many faithful begin quietly recollected, joining the priest interiorly as Mass starts.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Introibo ad altare Dei.
textBlocks.english: I will go unto the altar of God.

textBlocks.speaker: Server
textBlocks.latin: Ad Deum qui laetificat juventutem meam.
textBlocks.english: To God, who giveth joy to my youth.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest and Server
textBlocks.latin: Adjutorium nostrum in nomine Domini.
textBlocks.english: Our help is in the name of the Lord.

explanationNotes.title: Why the priest does not begin on the altar
explanationNotes.body: The Church begins with a spiritual preparation. Before the sacrifice is offered, priest and faithful ask God for mercy, purity, and recollection.

explanationNotes.title: How to participate if you are new
explanationNotes.body: You do not need to catch every word immediately. Follow the overall movement: preparation, listening, offering, consecration, communion, and thanksgiving.

explanationNotes.title: Why the rite begins below the altar
explanationNotes.body: The prayers at the foot of the altar underline that the sacrifice is approached, not assumed. The liturgy teaches humility before it teaches confidence.

parts.liveNote: Mass begins quietly here. If you lose your place early, rejoin the prayerful preparation and follow the posture of the faithful.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, the opening can feel very quiet. Use the posture of the faithful and the overall movement toward the altar to settle your attention.

formProfiles.summary: Mass begins with a more ceremonial pace. Let the ministers, chant, and gathering movement establish the beginning before trying to match every line.
formProfiles.liveNote: If this is a Sung Mass, the opening may take longer and feel more processional than conversational.
formProfiles.participationNote: You can participate fruitfully here by watching the altar and letting the ceremonial pace teach you the start of the rite.

gestureCues.label: Let the ceremonial pace settle in
gestureCues.detail: At a Sung Mass, the opening is often more extended before the priest reaches the center of the altar.

quickGuidance.title: Follow the broad movement first
quickGuidance.body: Let this opening teach the shape of the rite: preparation before sacrifice. You do not need every line before you can pray well.

quickGuidance.title: Local custom may differ
quickGuidance.body: Whether people stand or kneel here can vary. Calmly follow the congregation when you can and remain recollected when you cannot.

parts.title: Confiteor and Absolution Prayers
parts.summary: The Confiteor expresses repentance before entering more deeply into the sacred mysteries.

gestureCues.label: Bow at the mea culpa
gestureCues.detail: Many strike the breast lightly three times at the repeated confession of fault.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest or Server
textBlocks.latin: Confiteor Deo omnipotenti, beatae Mariae semper Virgini, beato Michaeli Archangelo... quia peccavi nimis cogitatione, verbo et opere: mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima culpa.
textBlocks.english: I confess to almighty God, to blessed Mary ever Virgin, to blessed Michael the Archangel... that I have sinned exceedingly in thought, word, and deed: through my fault, through my fault, through my most grievous fault.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Misereatur tui omnipotens Deus, et dimissis peccatis tuis, perducat te ad vitam aeternam.
textBlocks.english: May almighty God have mercy upon thee, forgive thee thy sins, and bring thee to life everlasting.

explanationNotes.title: Why this prayer is said twice
explanationNotes.body: The priest first confesses his own sinfulness; the ministers then answer and make the same confession. The repetition highlights that everyone needs mercy before the holy sacrifice.

parts.liveNote: This is still preparation for sacrifice. The repeated confession teaches recollection before the sacred action deepens.

parts.title: Kyrie and Gloria
parts.summary: After the preparation, Mass turns toward praise and petition.

gestureCues.label: Stand if the Gloria is said
gestureCues.detail: On some penitential days the Gloria is omitted, but the Kyrie remains.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest and Server
textBlocks.latin: Kyrie, eleison. Christe, eleison. Kyrie, eleison.
textBlocks.english: Lord, have mercy. Christ, have mercy. Lord, have mercy.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Gloria in excelsis Deo, et in terra pax hominibus bonae voluntatis.
textBlocks.english: Glory be to God on high, and on earth peace to men of good will.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Kyrie is in Greek
explanationNotes.body: The Roman liturgy retains this ancient Greek acclamation as a sign of the Church's continuity with early Christian worship.

explanationNotes.title: What the Gloria changes in the mood of Mass
explanationNotes.body: The Gloria moves from repentance into joyful praise. Its omission on certain days also teaches that the liturgy has seasons and varying tones.

parts.liveNote: The entrance prayers are giving way to the public praise of the Mass. Listen for the movement from mercy to worship.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, these prayers often move quickly. Hold onto the shift from penitence to praise rather than worrying about the exact pacing.

formProfiles.summary: In a Sung Mass, the Kyrie and Gloria often become major audible landmarks that slow the pace and make the liturgy easier to follow by ear.
formProfiles.liveNote: Listen for the schola or choir here. These are some of the clearest sung Ordinary landmarks in the whole Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: If you are new to Sung Mass, let the chant guide you more than the page. You can rejoin at the next stable heading without anxiety.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the sung Ordinary
gestureCues.detail: The Kyrie and Gloria are often among the easiest parts of a Sung Mass to recognize by ear.

parts.title: Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
parts.summary: The Church gathers the prayers of the faithful and then proclaims the first readings of the day.

gestureCues.label: Sit for the readings if local custom permits
gestureCues.detail: The proper texts of the Collect and Epistle change according to the day.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Dominus vobiscum.
textBlocks.english: The Lord be with you.

textBlocks.speaker: Server
textBlocks.latin: Et cum spiritu tuo.
textBlocks.english: And with thy spirit.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Oremus.
textBlocks.english: Let us pray.

explanationNotes.title: What the Collect does
explanationNotes.body: The Collect gathers the prayers of the Church into one brief petition that expresses the grace sought on that day. It tells you what the liturgy is asking from God before the readings unfold that same mystery more fully.

explanationNotes.title: Why some texts are not identical every day
explanationNotes.body: The readings and many prayers belong to the proper of the Mass and therefore vary with the feast, season, or saint being commemorated. This is one of the safest places to confirm the day without trying to decode every smaller variation at once.

explanationNotes.title: Why newcomers can trust this section
explanationNotes.body: Even when the chants or ceremonial details vary locally, the collect and readings remain one of the clearest places to recognize what day the Church is celebrating and what grace the liturgy is asking for.

parts.liveNote: This is one of the clearest places where the day changes. Proper texts often appear here first.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the clearest places where the day changes. If you are trying to reconnect after arriving late, start here.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than in a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: Use the Collect and the chant between the readings as a way to confirm the feast of the day and your place in the liturgy.

quickGuidance.title: Watch for what changes today
quickGuidance.body: This is one of the first places where the feast, season, or Sunday becomes obvious. If you arrived late, reconnect here.

quickGuidance.title: Ordinary and Propers meet here
quickGuidance.body: The greeting and shape stay familiar, but the collect and readings belong to the day. This is why the app treats this section as a major landmark and one of the safest places to reconnect calmly.

parts.title: Gospel
parts.summary: The Gospel is proclaimed with special reverence as the words and deeds of Christ are set before the faithful.

gestureCues.label: Stand for the Gospel
gestureCues.detail: Many make small signs of the cross on forehead, lips, and heart at the announcement of the Gospel.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Sequentia sancti Evangelii secundum Joannem.
textBlocks.english: The continuation of the holy Gospel according to John.

textBlocks.speaker: Server
textBlocks.latin: Gloria tibi, Domine.
textBlocks.english: Glory be to Thee, O Lord.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Gospel receives special signs of honor
explanationNotes.body: Standing, signing oneself, and attentive silence all express that Christ Himself teaches His people through the Gospel.

parts.liveNote: Stand with attention. The Gospel is a good point to rejoin the flow if you have lost your place.

parts.title: Credo
parts.summary: On many Sundays and feasts, the congregation professes the faith before the sacrificial part of Mass begins.

gestureCues.label: Bow at Et incarnatus est
gestureCues.detail: This gesture honors the mystery of the Incarnation.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Credo in unum Deum.
textBlocks.english: I believe in one God.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Et incarnatus est de Spiritu Sancto ex Maria Virgine: et homo factus est.
textBlocks.english: And was incarnate by the Holy Ghost of the Virgin Mary: and was made man.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Creed comes here
explanationNotes.body: Before moving to the offering and sacrifice, the Church openly confesses the faith that gives meaning to the entire liturgy.

parts.liveNote: When the Creed is said, the guide has moved through the teaching phase and toward the offering.

parts.title: Offertory
parts.summary: Bread and wine are set apart for sacrifice while the Church begins the offering of herself to God.

gestureCues.label: Sit or kneel according to local custom
gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is quieter and often accompanied by the server's responses.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Suscipe, sancte Pater, omnipotens aeterne Deus, hanc immaculatam hostiam...
textBlocks.english: Receive, O holy Father, almighty and eternal God, this spotless host...

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Offerimus tibi, Domine, calicem salutaris...
textBlocks.english: We offer unto Thee, O Lord, the chalice of salvation...

explanationNotes.title: What is happening at the Offertory
explanationNotes.body: The gifts are not yet consecrated. The Church is preparing matter for sacrifice and already uniting the faithful to Christ's coming self-offering. This is the moment to move from listening toward offering, not to confuse preparation with the consecration itself.

explanationNotes.title: The offertory prepares more than bread and wine
explanationNotes.body: Traditional missals often explain the Offertory as the Church’s first explicit drawing of the faithful into Christ’s self-offering. The gifts are prepared, and the people are prepared with them.

parts.liveNote: The gifts are being prepared. The app keeps the movement calm here so you can follow the offering without rushing.

formProfiles.participationNote: The Offertory is a helpful place to offer your own intentions quietly with the sacrifice being prepared.

formProfiles.summary: In a Sung Mass, the Offertory often opens up with chant and more visible ceremonial action, which can make the offering phase easier to notice.
formProfiles.liveNote: Watch the altar and listen for the offertory chant. This is a stable landmark before the Canon begins.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, let the offertory chant and ceremonial action slow you down rather than trying to force a fast page-by-page pace.

quickGuidance.title: Offer your intentions quietly
quickGuidance.body: The Church prepares the sacrificial gifts here. It is a fitting time to place your own intentions within the offering being made on the altar.

parts.title: Lavabo and Orate, Fratres
parts.summary: The priest washes his fingers and then turns to ask the people to pray that the sacrifice be acceptable.

gestureCues.label: Stand for Orate, fratres if local custom follows it
gestureCues.detail: The people remain united to the priest's offering through prayer, even when the server speaks aloud on their behalf.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Lavabo inter innocentes manus meas: et circumdabo altare tuum, Domine.
textBlocks.english: I will wash my hands among the innocent: and will compass Thine altar, O Lord.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Orate, fratres: ut meum ac vestrum sacrificium acceptabile fiat apud Deum Patrem omnipotentem.
textBlocks.english: Pray, brethren, that my sacrifice and yours may be acceptable to God the Father almighty.

textBlocks.speaker: Server
textBlocks.latin: Suscipiat Dominus sacrificium de manibus tuis...
textBlocks.english: May the Lord receive the sacrifice from thy hands...

explanationNotes.title: Why the priest washes his hands
explanationNotes.body: The washing symbolizes interior purity. The Church asks that the one who approaches the holy mysteries do so with a clean heart.

parts.liveNote: This transition prepares the sacrifice more intensely. Watch for posture changes and the invitation to prayer.

parts.title: Preface and Sanctus
parts.summary: The dialogue of thanksgiving leads into the Sanctus, where earth joins the worship of heaven.

gestureCues.label: Kneel after the Sanctus
gestureCues.detail: The Canon begins in a spirit of adoration and recollection.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Sursum corda.
textBlocks.english: Lift up your hearts.

textBlocks.speaker: Server
textBlocks.latin: Habemus ad Dominum.
textBlocks.english: We have them lifted up to the Lord.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest and Server
textBlocks.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth.
textBlocks.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts.

explanationNotes.title: What the Preface is doing
explanationNotes.body: The Preface names reasons for thanksgiving and prepares the faithful to enter the Canon. The Sanctus then joins earthly worship to the praise of the angels.

parts.liveNote: The Preface gathers the whole church toward the Canon. If bells or a deeper silence begin, the most sacred part is close.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is the last public threshold before the Canon. Let the Sanctus help you prepare for the silence of the Eucharistic prayer.

formProfiles.summary: At a Sung Mass, the Preface and Sanctus can become one of the clearest transitions into the Canon, carried by chant and a more solemn tempo.
formProfiles.liveNote: The sung Sanctus is often your last major sung landmark before the stillness of the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: If you lose your place, the Sanctus is one of the safest points to rejoin before the Canon deepens into silence.

quickGuidance.title: This is the last public threshold before the Canon
quickGuidance.body: The Preface gathers thanksgiving; the Sanctus prepares you for the stillness and adoration that follow.

parts.title: Canon of the Mass
parts.summary: The Roman Canon is prayed in a low voice as the Church remembers Christ's command and offers the spotless victim to the Father.

gestureCues.label: Remain kneeling in silence
gestureCues.detail: This part is marked by reverence and prayerful attention even when the words are not heard aloud.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Te igitur, clementissime Pater, per Jesum Christum Filium tuum Dominum nostrum supplices rogamus ac petimus...
textBlocks.english: Thee therefore, most merciful Father, through Jesus Christ Thy Son our Lord, we humbly pray and beseech...

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Quam oblationem tu, Deus, in omnibus, quaesumus, benedictam, adscriptam, ratam, rationabilem, acceptabilemque facere digneris...
textBlocks.english: Which offering do Thou, O God, vouchsafe in all things to make blessed, approved, ratified, reasonable, and acceptable...

explanationNotes.title: Why so much of the Canon is silent
explanationNotes.body: The hushed voice emphasizes awe before the sacred action. Silence is not emptiness here; it is a sign of mystery, adoration, and concentrated prayer. The faithful are meant to stay with the altar action and the sacrificial moment rather than force every prayer into audible form.

explanationNotes.title: Silence here is a theological sign, not a gap
explanationNotes.body: The hush of the Canon is part of the rite’s meaning. It marks the sacred action as something received in adoration, not treated like ordinary speech or instruction, and it keeps the center of the Mass from being reduced to explanation.

parts.liveNote: The Canon is the still center of the Mass. Stay with the altar action even when the priest speaks quietly.

quickGuidance.title: Do not chase every silent prayer
quickGuidance.body: The Canon is best followed by reverent attention to the altar action, not by anxious page-turning. Let the silence itself teach you what kind of moment this is, and let adoration outrun analysis.

quickGuidance.title: Stay with the altar action
quickGuidance.body: Watch the priest’s gestures, bows, and signs of the cross. They help you remain united to the sacrifice even when the words are inaudible.

parts.title: Consecration and Elevation
parts.summary: At the words of Christ, bread and wine become the Body and Blood of the Lord, and the Sacred Species are elevated for adoration.

gestureCues.label: Adore in silence
gestureCues.detail: Many look up at the Host and Chalice and make a quiet act of faith such as 'My Lord and my God.'

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Hoc est enim Corpus meum.
textBlocks.english: For this is My Body.
textBlocks.rubric: Words of consecration over the host

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Hic est enim Calix Sanguinis mei, novi et aeterni testamenti...
textBlocks.english: For this is the Chalice of My Blood, of the new and eternal testament...
textBlocks.rubric: Words of consecration over the chalice

explanationNotes.title: What Catholics believe happens here
explanationNotes.body: By the power of Christ working through the priest, the substance of bread and wine is changed into the Body and Blood of Christ. This is the heart of the Mass.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Sacred Species are elevated
explanationNotes.body: The elevation allows the faithful to adore Christ truly present and to unite themselves to the sacrifice made sacramentally present on the altar.

parts.liveNote: This is the heart of the sacrificial action. If you have lost the line, simply return to adoration here.

quickGuidance.title: Adore rather than analyze
quickGuidance.body: At the elevations, a short act of faith is often more fitting than trying to keep pace with the page.

parts.title: Pater Noster and Agnus Dei
parts.summary: The Lord's Prayer prepares for communion, and the Agnus Dei asks the Lamb of God for mercy and peace.

gestureCues.label: Strike the breast at the Agnus Dei
gestureCues.detail: This gesture expresses humility before receiving the Lord.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Pater noster, qui es in caelis, sanctificetur nomen tuum...
textBlocks.english: Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name...

textBlocks.speaker: Priest and Server
textBlocks.latin: Agnus Dei, qui tollis peccata mundi, miserere nobis.
textBlocks.english: Lamb of God, who takest away the sins of the world, have mercy on us.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest and Faithful
textBlocks.latin: Domine, non sum dignus ut intres sub tectum meum: sed tantum dic verbo, et sanabitur anima mea.
textBlocks.english: Lord, I am not worthy that Thou shouldst enter under my roof: but only say the word, and my soul shall be healed.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Our Father is placed here
explanationNotes.body: Having entered the sacrificial mystery, the Church now asks the Father for daily bread in the deepest sense and for deliverance from evil before communion.

parts.liveNote: The Mass is turning from consecration toward Communion. Familiar congregational responses often help users rejoin here.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the Agnus Dei often returns as a clear chant landmark after the silence of the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: This is a strong re-entry point after the Canon because the sung Agnus Dei is easier to recognize than many quieter prayers.

quickGuidance.title: Use the Agnus Dei as a re-entry point
quickGuidance.body: If the Canon felt hidden or difficult to follow, this is a natural place to regain your bearings before Communion.

parts.title: Communion
parts.summary: The priest communicates first, then distributes Holy Communion to the faithful when it is given during Mass.

gestureCues.label: Kneel and receive reverently if you are prepared to receive
gestureCues.detail: Non-Catholics or those not prepared for sacramental Communion may remain in prayer and make a spiritual communion.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Corpus Domini nostri Jesu Christi custodiat animam tuam in vitam aeternam. Amen.
textBlocks.english: May the Body of our Lord Jesus Christ preserve thy soul unto life everlasting. Amen.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir or Priest
textBlocks.latin: Communio.
textBlocks.english: Communion antiphon.

explanationNotes.title: Why reception is surrounded by humility
explanationNotes.body: Holy Communion is intimate union with Christ. The prayers before and after it teach wonder, gratitude, and the need to receive with faith and proper disposition. Even when you are not receiving sacramentally, this moment still invites humility, thanksgiving, and spiritual desire.

explanationNotes.title: Communion remains prayerful even when you stay in place
explanationNotes.body: Older hand missals regularly help the faithful remain united to the sacrifice through desire, thanksgiving, and spiritual communion when they are not receiving sacramentally. The point is union with Christ, not visible activity.

parts.liveNote: Communion texts and posture changes make this an easy place to find your bearings again.

formProfiles.participationNote: Communion is a calm place to rejoin the guide if you have lost your place earlier in Mass.

formProfiles.summary: At a Sung Mass, the Communion chant can help you rejoin the rite gently without rushing through the page.
formProfiles.liveNote: Listen for the Communion chant here. It often tells you where the liturgy has arrived even before you find the line.
formProfiles.participationNote: If you are following a Sung Mass, let the Communion chant and the movement of the faithful orient you first.

quickGuidance.title: Remain recollected even if you are not receiving
quickGuidance.body: The guide can help you pray the Communion rite whether you approach the altar rail or remain in your place for a spiritual communion. You are not outside the rite simply because you are remaining in prayer.

parts.title: Postcommunion, Dismissal, and Last Gospel
parts.summary: Mass closes with thanksgiving, dismissal, blessing, and the Last Gospel, usually the opening of Saint John's Gospel.

gestureCues.label: Stand for the blessing and Last Gospel
gestureCues.detail: Many make the sign of the cross at the final blessing and small crosses at the beginning of the Last Gospel.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Ite, missa est.
textBlocks.english: Go, the Mass is ended.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Benedicat vos omnipotens Deus, Pater, et Filius, et Spiritus Sanctus.
textBlocks.english: May almighty God bless you, the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Ghost.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: In principio erat Verbum, et Verbum erat apud Deum, et Deus erat Verbum.
textBlocks.english: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

explanationNotes.title: Why Mass ends with a sending
explanationNotes.body: The faithful are not dismissed from grace but sent with it. The Last Gospel leaves the mystery of the Incarnation ringing in the heart as one returns to daily life, so the rite closes with thanksgiving rather than with a merely practical ending.

parts.liveNote: The rite is concluding, but thanksgiving still matters. The guide keeps the final prayers visible instead of ending abruptly.

quickGuidance.title: Do not leave Mass interiorly too early
quickGuidance.body: The concluding prayers and Last Gospel keep the thanksgiving of the Mass from ending abruptly. Stay with them before turning to the day’s duties.

celebrations.title: Octave Day of Christmas
celebrations.subtitle: The Circumcision and Name of the Lord within Christmastide.
celebrations.summary: The year opens under the mystery of the Incarnation and the saving obedience of the Lord.

properSections.title: Octave Day of Christmas Entrance Proper, Kyrie, and Gloria
properSections.summary: The entrance proper for octave day of christmas establishes the character of the day before the liturgy moves into the sung or spoken Ordinary.

gestureCues.label: Notice the day's opening character
gestureCues.detail: The entrance proper is one of the clearest early signals that this day has its own liturgical accent.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Puer natus est nobis, et Filius datus est nobis.
textBlocks.english: A Child is born to us, and a Son is given to us.

textBlocks.latin: Octave Day of Christmas
textBlocks.english: The entrance proper frames the day before the Kyrie and Gloria move the Mass from penitence into praise.
textBlocks.rubric: How this day changes the opening

quickGuidance.title: Let the day announce itself
quickGuidance.body: The Introit is one of the clearest early signals that this Mass has its own accent. Hear the character of the day before trying to master the page.

quickGuidance.title: You can follow by landmarks
quickGuidance.body: Even if the opening feels unfamiliar, the entrance proper, Kyrie, and Gloria together form one dependable opening unit in the app.

explanationNotes.title: Why the opening proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Introit is one of the first places where the day announces itself. Even when you do not know every word, it helps fix the feast or season in your mind before the liturgy continues.

explanationNotes.title: What changes here from the fixed Ordinary
explanationNotes.body: For Octave Day of Christmas, the opening proper keeps the stable shape of the rite but gives it a distinct seasonal voice at the very beginning of Mass.

properSections.liveNote: The day-specific character is already visible here. This is an early place to confirm that you are on the right feast or Sunday.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, the opening proper is often easier to notice by its position in the rite than by constant page-tracking. Let it set the day's tone without anxiety.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper often carries the opening of the liturgy more than the page does.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper can be one of the easiest places to orient yourself by ear. Let the chant establish the day before you chase the text.

properSections.title: Octave Day of Christmas Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
properSections.summary: The year opens under the mystery of the Incarnation and the saving obedience of the Lord.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Deus, qui hodierna die Unigenitum tuum gentibus stella duce revelasti.
textBlocks.english: O God, who on this day hast revealed Thy Only-begotten Son to the nations.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Octave Day of Christmas
textBlocks.english: The liturgy keeps the faithful close to the mystery of the Word made flesh and the manifestation of Christ.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tecum principium in die virtutis tuae.
textBlocks.english: With Thee is the principality in the day of Thy strength.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day
quickGuidance.body: The collect and readings are among the safest points to reconnect if you are unsure what celebration is being observed.

quickGuidance.title: This is where the day-specific texts become concrete
quickGuidance.body: The app marks this section carefully because the collect, readings, and chant usually reveal the feast more clearly than the quieter opening prayers do.

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The year opens under the mystery of the Incarnation and the saving obedience of the Lord. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.

explanationNotes.title: Why this proper matters pastorally
explanationNotes.body: Traditional hand missals often teach newcomers to anchor themselves in the collect and readings because these are the moments when the Church says most plainly what grace she is asking for on that day.

properSections.liveNote: Proper texts usually become most obvious here. If you are following live, this is a reliable place to rejoin the day's celebration.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the safest places to reconnect with the day if you are new or if you arrived after Mass began.

formProfiles.liveNote: In a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than at a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the chant around the readings often helps you confirm both the feast and your place in the guide.

properSections.title: Octave Day of Christmas Offertory Proper
properSections.summary: The offertory proper for octave day of christmas turns the day's focus toward sacrifice and offering before the Canon begins.

gestureCues.label: Offer the day with the sacrifice
gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is a strong place to unite your own petitions with the offering being prepared on the altar.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tui sunt caeli, et tua est terra: orbem terrarum et plenitudinem eius tu fundasti.
textBlocks.english: Thine are the heavens, and thine is the earth: the world and the fulness thereof Thou hast founded.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Octave Day of Christmas
textBlocks.english: The offertory for this celebration deepens the day's emphasis: the year opens under the mystery of the incarnation and the saving obedience of the lord.

quickGuidance.title: Offer the day with the sacrifice
quickGuidance.body: This is a strong place to unite your own petitions to the offering being prepared, especially if you want to move from watching into prayer.

quickGuidance.title: Notice how the day’s emphasis returns
quickGuidance.body: The offertory proper gives the celebration another recognizable voice before the Canon begins.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory proper helps orient you
explanationNotes.body: The Offertory gives the faithful another dependable landmark between the readings and the Canon, especially on stronger feasts and Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory prepares the sacrifice
explanationNotes.body: The traditional offertory prayers and chant together shift the faithful from listening to offering. The day-specific proper keeps the feast in view as the rite turns toward the Canon.

properSections.liveNote: This proper-backed Offertory is a stable place to recover your bearings before the Canon begins.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, use the Offertory to offer your own intentions quietly and to prepare for the more hidden prayers that follow.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant is one of the clearest landmarks before the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant and ceremonial movement often make this transition easier to notice than at a Low Mass.

properSections.title: Octave Day of Christmas Communion Proper
properSections.summary: Representative Communion texts for octave day of christmas keep the user aligned with the day's focus during the Communion rite.

gestureCues.label: Return here as Communion begins
gestureCues.detail: If you lose your place, the Communion rite is another dependable point to regain your bearings.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Vidimus stellam eius in Oriente, et venimus cum muneribus adorare Dominum.
textBlocks.english: We have seen His star in the East, and have come with gifts to adore the Lord.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Octave Day of Christmas
textBlocks.english: This Communion proper keeps the faithful attentive to the year opens under the mystery of the incarnation and the saving obedience of the lord.

quickGuidance.title: Rejoin here without rushing
quickGuidance.body: Communion is one of the gentlest points to recover your place. Let the movement of the rite orient you before worrying about every line.

quickGuidance.title: The day’s focus returns at Communion
quickGuidance.body: The Communion proper keeps the celebration from fading after the Canon by bringing the feast’s central theme back into prayer.

explanationNotes.title: Why the app marks Communion separately
explanationNotes.body: A second proper-backed section gives the user another obvious liturgical landmark after the Canon, especially on feast days and strong seasonal Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Communion proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Communion antiphon and its surrounding prayers remind the faithful that reception and thanksgiving belong to the same mystery. The feast’s voice remains present here, not only at the readings.

properSections.liveNote: Communion is one of the calmest ways to regain your place in the app without rushing ahead of the liturgy.

textBlocks.latin: Concede nobis, quaesumus, omnipotens Deus, ut per annua quadragesimalis exercitia sacramenti.
textBlocks.english: Grant us, we beseech Thee, almighty God, through the yearly exercises of this sacred season.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Septuagesima Sunday
textBlocks.english: The proper texts press the faithful toward repentance, steadiness, and deeper union with Christ's Passion.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tribulationes cordis mei dilatatae sunt; de necessitatibus meis erue me, Domine.
textBlocks.english: The troubles of my heart are enlarged; deliver me, O Lord, from my necessities.

quickGuidance.body: The Introit is one of the clearest early signals that this Mass has its own accent. Hear the character of the day before trying to master the page.

quickGuidance.title: You can follow by landmarks
quickGuidance.body: Even if the opening feels unfamiliar, the entrance proper, Kyrie, and Gloria together form one dependable opening unit in the app.

explanationNotes.title: Why the opening proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Introit is one of the first places where the day announces itself. Even when you do not know every word, it helps fix the feast or season in your mind before the liturgy continues.

explanationNotes.title: What changes here from the fixed Ordinary
explanationNotes.body: For Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary, the opening proper keeps the stable shape of the rite but gives it a distinct seasonal voice at the very beginning of Mass.

properSections.liveNote: The day-specific character is already visible here. This is an early place to confirm that you are on the right feast or Sunday.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, the opening proper is often easier to notice by its position in the rite than by constant page-tracking. Let it set the day's tone without anxiety.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper often carries the opening of the liturgy more than the page does.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper can be one of the easiest places to orient yourself by ear. Let the chant establish the day before you chase the text.

properSections.title: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
properSections.summary: The proper texts honor the guardian of the Redeemer and teach hidden fidelity.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Concede nobis, quaesumus, omnipotens Deus, ut per annua quadragesimalis exercitia sacramenti.
textBlocks.english: Grant us, we beseech Thee, almighty God, through the yearly exercises of this sacred season.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary
textBlocks.english: The proper texts press the faithful toward repentance, steadiness, and deeper union with Christ's Passion.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tribulationes cordis mei dilatatae sunt; de necessitatibus meis erue me, Domine.
textBlocks.english: The troubles of my heart are enlarged; deliver me, O Lord, from my necessities.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day
quickGuidance.body: The collect and readings are among the safest points to reconnect if you are unsure what celebration is being observed.

quickGuidance.title: This is where the day-specific texts become concrete
quickGuidance.body: The app marks this section carefully because the collect, readings, and chant usually reveal the feast more clearly than the quieter opening prayers do.

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The proper texts honor the guardian of the Redeemer and teach hidden fidelity. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.

explanationNotes.title: Why this proper matters pastorally
explanationNotes.body: Traditional hand missals often teach newcomers to anchor themselves in the collect and readings because these are the moments when the Church says most plainly what grace she is asking for on that day.

properSections.liveNote: Proper texts usually become most obvious here. If you are following live, this is a reliable place to rejoin the day's celebration.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the safest places to reconnect with the day if you are new or if you arrived after Mass began.

formProfiles.liveNote: In a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than at a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the chant around the readings often helps you confirm both the feast and your place in the guide.

properSections.title: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary Offertory Proper
properSections.summary: The offertory proper for saint joseph, spouse of the blessed virgin mary turns the day's focus toward sacrifice and offering before the Canon begins.

gestureCues.label: Offer the day with the sacrifice
gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is a strong place to unite your own petitions with the offering being prepared on the altar.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Meditabor in mandatis tuis, quae dilexi valde: et levabo manus meas ad mandata tua.
textBlocks.english: I will meditate on Thy commandments, which I have loved exceedingly: and I will lift up my hands to Thy commandments.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary
textBlocks.english: The offertory for this celebration deepens the day's emphasis: the proper texts honor the guardian of the redeemer and teach hidden fidelity.

quickGuidance.title: Offer the day with the sacrifice
quickGuidance.body: This is a strong place to unite your own petitions to the offering being prepared, especially if you want to move from watching into prayer.

quickGuidance.title: Notice how the day’s emphasis returns
quickGuidance.body: The offertory proper gives the celebration another recognizable voice before the Canon begins.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory proper helps orient you
explanationNotes.body: The Offertory gives the faithful another dependable landmark between the readings and the Canon, especially on stronger feasts and Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory prepares the sacrifice
explanationNotes.body: The traditional offertory prayers and chant together shift the faithful from listening to offering. The day-specific proper keeps the feast in view as the rite turns toward the Canon.

properSections.liveNote: This proper-backed Offertory is a stable place to recover your bearings before the Canon begins.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, use the Offertory to offer your own intentions quietly and to prepare for the more hidden prayers that follow.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant is one of the clearest landmarks before the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant and ceremonial movement often make this transition easier to notice than at a Low Mass.

properSections.title: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary Communion Proper
properSections.summary: Representative Communion texts for saint joseph, spouse of the blessed virgin mary keep the user aligned with the day's focus during the Communion rite.

gestureCues.label: Return here as Communion begins
gestureCues.detail: If you lose your place, the Communion rite is another dependable point to regain your bearings.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Qui meditabitur in lege Domini die ac nocte, dabit fructum suum in tempore suo.
textBlocks.english: He that shall meditate on the law of the Lord day and night shall yield his fruit in due season.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Saint Joseph, Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary
textBlocks.english: This Communion proper keeps the faithful attentive to the proper texts honor the guardian of the redeemer and teach hidden fidelity.

quickGuidance.title: Rejoin here without rushing
quickGuidance.body: Communion is one of the gentlest points to recover your place. Let the movement of the rite orient you before worrying about every line.

quickGuidance.title: The day’s focus returns at Communion
quickGuidance.body: The Communion proper keeps the celebration from fading after the Canon by bringing the feast’s central theme back into prayer.

explanationNotes.title: Why the app marks Communion separately
explanationNotes.body: A second proper-backed section gives the user another obvious liturgical landmark after the Canon, especially on feast days and strong seasonal Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Communion proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Communion antiphon and its surrounding prayers remind the faithful that reception and thanksgiving belong to the same mystery. The feast’s voice remains present here, not only at the readings.

properSections.liveNote: Communion is one of the calmest ways to regain your place in the app without rushing ahead of the liturgy.

formProfiles.participationNote: If you are not receiving Communion, this is still a fitting place to remain recollected and make a spiritual communion.

formProfiles.liveNote: The Communion chant often becomes a clear landmark in a Sung Mass, even when you are not tracking every line in real time.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the Communion chant can help you rejoin the rite gently even if the earlier pacing felt unfamiliar.

celebrations.title: Passion Sunday
celebrations.subtitle: Passiontide begins.
celebrations.summary: The Church turns more directly toward the Passion and keeps the faithful close to the coming sacrifice.

properSections.title: Passion Sunday Entrance Proper, Kyrie, and Gloria
properSections.summary: The entrance proper for passion sunday establishes the character of the day before the liturgy moves into the sung or spoken Ordinary.

gestureCues.label: Notice the day's opening character
gestureCues.detail: The entrance proper is one of the clearest early signals that this day has its own liturgical accent.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Invocabit me, et ego exaudiam eum: eripiam eum et glorificabo eum.
textBlocks.english: He shall cry to Me, and I will hear him: I will rescue him and glorify him.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Passion Sunday
textBlocks.english: The entrance proper frames the day before the Kyrie and Gloria move the Mass from penitence into praise.

celebrations.subtitle: Forty days after Easter.
celebrations.summary: The Lord ascends in glory, and the Church learns to long for heavenly things.

properSections.title: Ascension of the Lord Entrance Proper, Kyrie, and Gloria
properSections.summary: The entrance proper for ascension of the lord establishes the character of the day before the liturgy moves into the sung or spoken Ordinary.

gestureCues.label: Notice the day's opening character
gestureCues.detail: The entrance proper is one of the clearest early signals that this day has its own liturgical accent.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Resurrexi, et adhuc tecum sum, alleluia.
textBlocks.english: I arose, and am still with Thee, alleluia.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Ascension of the Lord
textBlocks.english: The entrance proper frames the day before the Kyrie and Gloria move the Mass from penitence into praise.
textBlocks.rubric: How this day changes the opening

quickGuidance.title: Let the day announce itself
quickGuidance.body: The Introit is one of the clearest early signals that this Mass has its own accent. Hear the character of the day before trying to master the page.

quickGuidance.title: You can follow by landmarks
quickGuidance.body: Even if the opening feels unfamiliar, the entrance proper, Kyrie, and Gloria together form one dependable opening unit in the app.

explanationNotes.title: Why the opening proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Introit is one of the first places where the day announces itself. Even when you do not know every word, it helps fix the feast or season in your mind before the liturgy continues.

explanationNotes.title: What changes here from the fixed Ordinary
explanationNotes.body: For Ascension of the Lord, the opening proper keeps the stable shape of the rite but gives it a distinct seasonal voice at the very beginning of Mass.

properSections.liveNote: The day-specific character is already visible here. This is an early place to confirm that you are on the right feast or Sunday.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, the opening proper is often easier to notice by its position in the rite than by constant page-tracking. Let it set the day's tone without anxiety.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper often carries the opening of the liturgy more than the page does.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper can be one of the easiest places to orient yourself by ear. Let the chant establish the day before you chase the text.

properSections.title: Ascension of the Lord Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
properSections.summary: The Lord ascends in glory, and the Church learns to long for heavenly things.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Deus, qui hodierna die per Unigenitum tuum aeternitatis nobis aditum devicta morte reserasti.
textBlocks.english: O God, who on this day by Thy Only-begotten Son hast conquered death and opened to us the gate of eternity.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Ascension of the Lord
textBlocks.english: The Paschal cycle teaches resurrection joy, confidence in grace, and the life of the Church after Easter.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Haec dies quam fecit Dominus; exsultemus et laetemur in ea.
textBlocks.english: This is the day which the Lord hath made; let us rejoice and be glad in it.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day
quickGuidance.body: The collect and readings are among the safest points to reconnect if you are unsure what celebration is being observed.

quickGuidance.title: This is where the day-specific texts become concrete
quickGuidance.body: The app marks this section carefully because the collect, readings, and chant usually reveal the feast more clearly than the quieter opening prayers do.

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The Lord ascends in glory, and the Church learns to long for heavenly things. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.

explanationNotes.title: Why this proper matters pastorally
explanationNotes.body: Traditional hand missals often teach newcomers to anchor themselves in the collect and readings because these are the moments when the Church says most plainly what grace she is asking for on that day.

properSections.liveNote: Proper texts usually become most obvious here. If you are following live, this is a reliable place to rejoin the day's celebration.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the safest places to reconnect with the day if you are new or if you arrived after Mass began.

formProfiles.liveNote: In a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than at a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the chant around the readings often helps you confirm both the feast and your place in the guide.

properSections.title: Ascension of the Lord Offertory Proper
properSections.summary: The offertory proper for ascension of the lord turns the day's focus toward sacrifice and offering before the Canon begins.

gestureCues.label: Offer the day with the sacrifice
gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is a strong place to unite your own petitions with the offering being prepared on the altar.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Terra tremuit et quievit, dum resurgeret in iudicium Deus, alleluia.
textBlocks.english: The earth trembled and was still when God arose in judgment, alleluia.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Ascension of the Lord
textBlocks.english: The offertory for this celebration deepens the day's emphasis: the lord ascends in glory, and the church learns to long for heavenly things.

quickGuidance.title: Offer the day with the sacrifice
quickGuidance.body: This is a strong place to unite your own petitions to the offering being prepared, especially if you want to move from watching into prayer.

quickGuidance.title: Notice how the day’s emphasis returns
quickGuidance.body: The offertory proper gives the celebration another recognizable voice before the Canon begins.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory proper helps orient you
explanationNotes.body: The Offertory gives the faithful another dependable landmark between the readings and the Canon, especially on stronger feasts and Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory prepares the sacrifice
explanationNotes.body: The traditional offertory prayers and chant together shift the faithful from listening to offering. The day-specific proper keeps the feast in view as the rite turns toward the Canon.

properSections.liveNote: This proper-backed Offertory is a stable place to recover your bearings before the Canon begins.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, use the Offertory to offer your own intentions quietly and to prepare for the more hidden prayers that follow.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant is one of the clearest landmarks before the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant and ceremonial movement often make this transition easier to notice than at a Low Mass.

properSections.title: Ascension of the Lord Communion Proper
properSections.summary: Representative Communion texts for ascension of the lord keep the user aligned with the day's focus during the Communion rite.

gestureCues.label: Return here as Communion begins
gestureCues.detail: If you lose your place, the Communion rite is another dependable point to regain your bearings.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Pascha nostrum immolatus est Christus, alleluia.
textBlocks.english: Christ our Pasch is sacrificed, alleluia.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Ascension of the Lord
textBlocks.english: This Communion proper keeps the faithful attentive to the lord ascends in glory, and the church learns to long for heavenly things.

quickGuidance.title: Rejoin here without rushing
quickGuidance.body: Communion is one of the gentlest points to recover your place. Let the movement of the rite orient you before worrying about every line.

quickGuidance.title: The day’s focus returns at Communion
quickGuidance.body: The Communion proper keeps the celebration from fading after the Canon by bringing the feast’s central theme back into prayer.

explanationNotes.title: Why the app marks Communion separately
explanationNotes.body: A second proper-backed section gives the user another obvious liturgical landmark after the Canon, especially on feast days and strong seasonal Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Communion proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Communion antiphon and its surrounding prayers remind the faithful that reception and thanksgiving belong to the same mystery. The feast’s voice remains present here, not only at the readings.

properSections.liveNote: Communion is one of the calmest ways to regain your place in the app without rushing ahead of the liturgy.

formProfiles.participationNote: If you are not receiving Communion, this is still a fitting place to remain recollected and make a spiritual communion.

formProfiles.liveNote: The Communion chant often becomes a clear landmark in a Sung Mass, even when you are not tracking every line in real time.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the Communion chant can help you rejoin the rite gently even if the earlier pacing felt unfamiliar.

celebrations.title: Trinity Sunday
celebrations.subtitle: The mystery of the Blessed Trinity.
celebrations.summary: The Mass turns from Paschal fulfillment to adoration of the Most Holy Trinity.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Benedicta sit sancta Trinitas atque indivisa Unitas: confitebimur ei, quia fecit nobiscum misericordiam suam.
textBlocks.english: Blessed be the Holy Trinity and undivided Unity: we will praise Him because He has shown us His mercy.

properSections.summary: The Mass turns from Paschal fulfillment to adoration of the Most Holy Trinity.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Praesta, quaesumus, omnipotens et misericors Deus, ut quae tibi placita sunt et te inspirante cogitemus.
textBlocks.english: Grant, we beseech Thee, almighty and merciful God, that we may think those things that please Thee.

textBlocks.latin: Trinity Sunday
textBlocks.english: The long green season teaches perseverance, ordered charity, gratitude, and readiness for the Lord's return.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Bonum est confiteri Domino, et psallere nomini tuo, Altissime.
textBlocks.english: It is good to give praise to the Lord, and to sing to Thy name, O Most High.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day
quickGuidance.body: The collect and readings are among the safest points to reconnect if you are unsure what celebration is being observed.

quickGuidance.title: This is where the day-specific texts become concrete
quickGuidance.body: The app marks this section carefully because the collect, readings, and chant usually reveal the feast more clearly than the quieter opening prayers do.

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The Mass turns from Paschal fulfillment to adoration of the Most Holy Trinity. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.

explanationNotes.title: Why this proper matters pastorally
explanationNotes.body: Traditional hand missals often teach newcomers to anchor themselves in the collect and readings because these are the moments when the Church says most plainly what grace she is asking for on that day.

properSections.liveNote: Proper texts usually become most obvious here. If you are following live, this is a reliable place to rejoin the day's celebration.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the safest places to reconnect with the day if you are new or if you arrived after Mass began.

formProfiles.liveNote: In a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than at a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the chant around the readings often helps you confirm both the feast and your place in the guide.

properSections.title: Trinity Sunday Offertory Proper
properSections.summary: The offertory proper for trinity sunday turns the day's focus toward sacrifice and offering before the Canon begins.

gestureCues.label: Offer the day with the sacrifice
gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is a strong place to unite your own petitions with the offering being prepared on the altar.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Benedictus sit Deus Pater, unigenitusque Dei Filius, sanctus quoque Spiritus.
textBlocks.english: Blessed be God the Father, and the Only-begotten Son of God, and the Holy Ghost.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Trinity Sunday
textBlocks.english: The offertory for this celebration deepens the day's emphasis: the mass turns from paschal fulfillment to adoration of the most holy trinity.

quickGuidance.title: Offer the day with the sacrifice
quickGuidance.body: This is a strong place to unite your own petitions to the offering being prepared, especially if you want to move from watching into prayer.

quickGuidance.title: Notice how the day’s emphasis returns
quickGuidance.body: The offertory proper gives the celebration another recognizable voice before the Canon begins.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory proper helps orient you
explanationNotes.body: The Offertory gives the faithful another dependable landmark between the readings and the Canon, especially on stronger feasts and Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory prepares the sacrifice
explanationNotes.body: The traditional offertory prayers and chant together shift the faithful from listening to offering. The day-specific proper keeps the feast in view as the rite turns toward the Canon.

properSections.liveNote: This proper-backed Offertory is a stable place to recover your bearings before the Canon begins.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, use the Offertory to offer your own intentions quietly and to prepare for the more hidden prayers that follow.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant is one of the clearest landmarks before the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant and ceremonial movement often make this transition easier to notice than at a Low Mass.

properSections.title: Trinity Sunday Communion Proper
properSections.summary: Representative Communion texts for trinity sunday keep the user aligned with the day's focus during the Communion rite.

gestureCues.label: Return here as Communion begins
gestureCues.detail: If you lose your place, the Communion rite is another dependable point to regain your bearings.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Panem de caelo dedisti nobis, Domine, habentem omne delectamentum.
textBlocks.english: Thou hast given us bread from heaven, O Lord, having in it all delight.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Trinity Sunday
textBlocks.english: This Communion proper keeps the faithful attentive to the mass turns from paschal fulfillment to adoration of the most holy trinity.

quickGuidance.title: Rejoin here without rushing
quickGuidance.body: Communion is one of the gentlest points to recover your place. Let the movement of the rite orient you before worrying about every line.

quickGuidance.title: The day’s focus returns at Communion
quickGuidance.body: The Communion proper keeps the celebration from fading after the Canon by bringing the feast’s central theme back into prayer.

explanationNotes.title: Why the app marks Communion separately
explanationNotes.body: A second proper-backed section gives the user another obvious liturgical landmark after the Canon, especially on feast days and strong seasonal Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Communion proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Communion antiphon and its surrounding prayers remind the faithful that reception and thanksgiving belong to the same mystery. The feast’s voice remains present here, not only at the readings.

properSections.liveNote: Communion is one of the calmest ways to regain your place in the app without rushing ahead of the liturgy.

celebrations.title: Corpus Christi

celebrations.summary: The Church dwells with special clarity on the gift of the Eucharistic Lord.

properSections.title: Corpus Christi Entrance Proper, Kyrie, and Gloria
properSections.summary: The entrance proper for corpus christi establishes the character of the day before the liturgy moves into the sung or spoken Ordinary.

gestureCues.label: Notice the day's opening character
gestureCues.detail: The entrance proper is one of the clearest early signals that this day has its own liturgical accent.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Benedicta sit sancta Trinitas atque indivisa Unitas: confitebimur ei, quia fecit nobiscum misericordiam suam.
textBlocks.english: Blessed be the Holy Trinity and undivided Unity: we will praise Him because He has shown us His mercy.

explanationNotes.title: Why the opening proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Introit is one of the first places where the day announces itself. Even when you do not know every word, it helps fix the feast or season in your mind before the liturgy continues.

properSections.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost Collect, Epistle, and Gradual

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest

textBlocks.english: Grant, we beseech Thee, almighty and merciful God, that we may think those things that please Thee.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost
textBlocks.english: The long green season teaches perseverance, ordered charity, gratitude, and readiness for the Lord's return.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary

properSections.title: 21st Sunday after Pentecost Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
properSections.summary: The Church continues the long green season with proper texts that teach perseverance, gratitude, and readiness for the Lord’s return.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Praesta, quaesumus, omnipotens et misericors Deus, ut quae tibi placita sunt et te inspirante cogitemus.
textBlocks.english: Grant, we beseech Thee, almighty and merciful God, that we may think those things that please Thee.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: 21st Sunday after Pentecost
textBlocks.english: The long green season teaches perseverance, ordered charity, gratitude, and readiness for the Lord's return.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Bonum est confiteri Domino, et psallere nomini tuo, Altissime.
textBlocks.english: It is good to give praise to the Lord, and to sing to Thy name, O Most High.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The Church continues the long green season with proper texts that teach perseverance, gratitude, and readiness for the Lord’s return. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.
