gestureCues.detail: The Offertory is a strong place to unite your own petitions with the offering being prepared on the altar.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tui sunt caeli, et tua est terra: orbem terrarum et plenitudinem eius tu fundasti.
textBlocks.english: Thine are the heavens, and thine is the earth: the world and the fulness thereof Thou hast founded.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Christmas Day
textBlocks.english: The offertory for this celebration deepens the day's emphasis: the mass rejoices in the word made flesh and the humility of the savior’s birth.

quickGuidance.title: Offer the day with the sacrifice
quickGuidance.body: This is a strong place to unite your own petitions to the offering being prepared, especially if you want to move from watching into prayer.

quickGuidance.title: Notice how the day’s emphasis returns
quickGuidance.body: The offertory proper gives the celebration another recognizable voice before the Canon begins.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory proper helps orient you
explanationNotes.body: The Offertory gives the faithful another dependable landmark between the readings and the Canon, especially on stronger feasts and Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the offertory prepares the sacrifice
explanationNotes.body: The traditional offertory prayers and chant together shift the faithful from listening to offering. The day-specific proper keeps the feast in view as the rite turns toward the Canon.

properSections.liveNote: This proper-backed Offertory is a stable place to recover your bearings before the Canon begins.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, use the Offertory to offer your own intentions quietly and to prepare for the more hidden prayers that follow.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant is one of the clearest landmarks before the Canon.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the offertory chant and ceremonial movement often make this transition easier to notice than at a Low Mass.

properSections.title: Christmas Day Communion Proper
properSections.summary: Representative Communion texts for christmas day keep the user aligned with the day's focus during the Communion rite.

gestureCues.label: Return here as Communion begins
gestureCues.detail: If you lose your place, the Communion rite is another dependable point to regain your bearings.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Vidimus stellam eius in Oriente, et venimus cum muneribus adorare Dominum.
textBlocks.english: We have seen His star in the East, and have come with gifts to adore the Lord.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Christmas Day
textBlocks.english: This Communion proper keeps the faithful attentive to the mass rejoices in the word made flesh and the humility of the savior’s birth.

quickGuidance.title: Rejoin here without rushing
quickGuidance.body: Communion is one of the gentlest points to recover your place. Let the movement of the rite orient you before worrying about every line.

quickGuidance.title: The day’s focus returns at Communion
quickGuidance.body: The Communion proper keeps the celebration from fading after the Canon by bringing the feast’s central theme back into prayer.

explanationNotes.title: Why the app marks Communion separately
explanationNotes.body: A second proper-backed section gives the user another obvious liturgical landmark after the Canon, especially on feast days and strong seasonal Sundays.

explanationNotes.title: Why the Communion proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Communion antiphon and its surrounding prayers remind the faithful that reception and thanksgiving belong to the same mystery. The feast’s voice remains present here, not only at the readings.

properSections.liveNote: Communion is one of the calmest ways to regain your place in the app without rushing ahead of the liturgy.

formProfiles.participationNote: If you are not receiving Communion, this is still a fitting place to remain recollected and make a spiritual communion.

formProfiles.liveNote: The Communion chant often becomes a clear landmark in a Sung Mass, even when you are not tracking every line in real time.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the Communion chant can help you rejoin the rite gently even if the earlier pacing felt unfamiliar.

celebrations.title: Sunday within the Octave of Christmas
celebrations.subtitle: Christmastide Sunday
celebrations.summary: The Church remains close to the mystery of the Nativity and keeps Christmas thanksgiving alive.

properSections.title: Sunday within the Octave of Christmas Entrance Proper, Kyrie, and Gloria
properSections.summary: The entrance proper for sunday within the octave of christmas establishes the character of the day before the liturgy moves into the sung or spoken Ordinary.

gestureCues.label: Notice the day's opening character
gestureCues.detail: The entrance proper is one of the clearest early signals that this day has its own liturgical accent.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Puer natus est nobis, et Filius datus est nobis.
textBlocks.english: A Child is born to us, and a Son is given to us.

textBlocks.speaker: Guide
textBlocks.latin: Sunday within the Octave of Christmas
textBlocks.english: The entrance proper frames the day before the Kyrie and Gloria move the Mass from penitence into praise.
textBlocks.rubric: How this day changes the opening

quickGuidance.title: Let the day announce itself
quickGuidance.body: The Introit is one of the clearest early signals that this Mass has its own accent. Hear the character of the day before trying to master the page.

quickGuidance.title: You can follow by landmarks
quickGuidance.body: Even if the opening feels unfamiliar, the entrance proper, Kyrie, and Gloria together form one dependable opening unit in the app.

explanationNotes.title: Why the opening proper matters
explanationNotes.body: The Introit is one of the first places where the day announces itself. Even when you do not know every word, it helps fix the feast or season in your mind before the liturgy continues.

explanationNotes.title: What changes here from the fixed Ordinary
explanationNotes.body: For Sunday within the Octave of Christmas, the opening proper keeps the stable shape of the rite but gives it a distinct seasonal voice at the very beginning of Mass.

properSections.liveNote: The day-specific character is already visible here. This is an early place to confirm that you are on the right feast or Sunday.

formProfiles.participationNote: At a Low Mass, the opening proper is often easier to notice by its position in the rite than by constant page-tracking. Let it set the day's tone without anxiety.

formProfiles.liveNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper often carries the opening of the liturgy more than the page does.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the entrance proper can be one of the easiest places to orient yourself by ear. Let the chant establish the day before you chase the text.

properSections.title: Sunday within the Octave of Christmas Collect, Epistle, and Gradual
properSections.summary: The Church remains close to the mystery of the Nativity and keeps Christmas thanksgiving alive.

gestureCues.label: Listen for the day's proper texts
gestureCues.detail: The collect and readings are one of the clearest places where the celebration changes.

textBlocks.speaker: Priest
textBlocks.latin: Deus, qui hodierna die Unigenitum tuum gentibus stella duce revelasti.
textBlocks.english: O God, who on this day hast revealed Thy Only-begotten Son to the nations.

textBlocks.speaker: Epistle Theme
textBlocks.latin: Sunday within the Octave of Christmas
textBlocks.english: The liturgy keeps the faithful close to the mystery of the Word made flesh and the manifestation of Christ.

textBlocks.speaker: Choir
textBlocks.latin: Tecum principium in die virtutis tuae.
textBlocks.english: With Thee is the principality in the day of Thy strength.

quickGuidance.title: Use this section to verify the day
quickGuidance.body: The collect and readings are among the safest points to reconnect if you are unsure what celebration is being observed.

quickGuidance.title: This is where the day-specific texts become concrete
quickGuidance.body: The app marks this section carefully because the collect, readings, and chant usually reveal the feast more clearly than the quieter opening prayers do.

explanationNotes.title: How this day changes the guide
explanationNotes.body: The Church remains close to the mystery of the Nativity and keeps Christmas thanksgiving alive. This section carries one of the most visible day-specific shifts in the app's live guide.

explanationNotes.title: Why this proper matters pastorally
explanationNotes.body: Traditional hand missals often teach newcomers to anchor themselves in the collect and readings because these are the moments when the Church says most plainly what grace she is asking for on that day.

properSections.liveNote: Proper texts usually become most obvious here. If you are following live, this is a reliable place to rejoin the day's celebration.

formProfiles.participationNote: This is one of the safest places to reconnect with the day if you are new or if you arrived after Mass began.

formProfiles.liveNote: In a Sung Mass, the Gradual or Alleluia may carry the transition into the Gospel more audibly than at a Low Mass.
formProfiles.participationNote: At a Sung Mass, the chant around the readings often helps you confirm both the feast and your place in the guide.

glossaryEntries.definition: The fixed prayers and ceremonies that stay substantially the same from day to day, even when the feast and propers change.
The prayers and chants that change according to the day, season, or feast, especially around the readings, offertory, and Communion.
A brief prayer that gathers the petitions of the Church into one focused plea proper to the day or feast.
The central Eucharistic prayer of the traditional Roman rite, prayed quietly as the sacrifice moves toward consecration.
The invocation 'Lamb of God' said before Communion to ask Christ for mercy and peace.

pronunciationGuides.title: Et cum spiritu tuo
pronunciationGuides.note: Keep the vowels clean and the phrase even. In church Latin, the final 'u-o' is usually heard as two light syllables.

pronunciationGuides.title: Mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima culpa
pronunciationGuides.latin: Mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima culpa.
pronunciationGuides.phonetic: MEH-ah KOOL-pah, MEH-ah KOOL-pah, MEH-ah MAX-ee-mah KOOL-pah.
pronunciationGuides.note: This is said with humility in the Confiteor. Keep the stress gentle rather than dramatic.

pronunciationGuides.title: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus
pronunciationGuides.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus Dominus Deus Sabaoth.
pronunciationGuides.phonetic: SAHNK-toos, SAHNK-toos, SAHNK-toos DOH-mee-noos DEH-oos SAH-bah-oht.
pronunciationGuides.note: If you are following a Sung Mass, this is one of the most recognizable chant landmarks. Keep the consonants light and the vowels steady.

pronunciationGuides.title: Domine, non sum dignus
pronunciationGuides.latin: Domine, non sum dignus ut intres sub tectum meum.
pronunciationGuides.phonetic: DOH-mee-neh, non soom DEEN-yoos oot EEN-tres soob TEHK-toom MEH-oom.
pronunciationGuides.note: This is a humble Communion prayer. Say it slowly enough that the sense of reverence is not lost.

participationGuides.title: First Time at the 1962 Mass
participationGuides.body: Do not try to master every word at once. Learn the broad movement of the Mass first: preparation, instruction, offering, canon, communion, and thanksgiving. The guide is meant to help you stay recollected, not to turn prayer into a race. If you can recognize what is happening, why the rite is turning there, and where the altar action is leading, you are already using the app well.

participationGuides.title: How to Use This App Well
participationGuides.body: Treat the app as a companion, not a scoreboard. Use Guide when you need to follow the movement of the rite. Use the form switch and jump controls to recover quickly. Use Library when you know what you want to find or want to reopen Bookmarks. Use Learn before or after Mass so the liturgy itself can remain primary while you are in church. If the phone begins to pull more attention than the altar, set it down and return by landmarks later.
searchAliases: how do i use this app

participationGuides.title: A Brief History and Context
participationGuides.body: The Traditional Latin Mass is the Roman rite as handed on in the liturgical books used before the postconciliar reform, especially the 1962 Missal. This app is not trying to teach the whole history of the rite. It offers just enough context to help newcomers understand why the older form has its own cadence, ceremonial language, and devotional expectations.
searchAliases: what is the traditional latin mass

participationGuides.title: Local Customs May Vary
participationGuides.body: Posture, spoken responses, and the exact rhythm of congregation participation can vary from place to place. Follow the local custom calmly when you can. When in doubt, pray quietly and let the liturgy teach you over time. The app is designed to orient you inside the traditional rite, not to overrule a parish's lawful custom from the pew.
searchAliases: what if people do something different

participationGuides.title: When You Lose Your Place
participationGuides.body: Do not panic and do not sprint through the page. Rejoin by landmarks: the current posture, the broad phase of the rite, a familiar heading such as Gospel, Offertory, Sanctus, or Communion, and the altar action in front of you. Missing a line is not the same as missing the Mass. Recollection matters more than recovery speed.

participationGuides.title: What Changes by Day
participationGuides.body: The Ordinary stays substantially fixed, while the propers change according to the feast, season, or Sunday. In this app, the clearest day-specific landmarks usually appear around the entrance proper, the collect and readings, the offertory, and Communion. When no bundled proper is available, the app keeps the Ordinary visible rather than guessing missing texts. That restraint is part of the app's trust promise, not a hidden failure.

participationGuides.title: Low Mass and Sung Mass
participationGuides.body: The structure of the rite remains recognizable in both forms, but Sung Mass is often slower, more ceremonial, and more chant-led. Low Mass usually leaves more of the audible response to the servers and asks the faithful to stay recollected amid quieter movement. The app lets you choose the form directly in the guide and library so it can emphasize either quieter landmarks or sung ones without pretending every parish does things identically.
searchAliases: difference between low and sung mass

participationGuides.title: How to Participate at a Low Mass
participationGuides.body: Follow the overall shape first: preparation, readings, offering, consecration, communion, thanksgiving. You do not need to catch every word on the first visit. If you lose your place, return to the current posture, then find the nearest section title in the guide. Quiet attention often serves better here than constant page-checking. At Low Mass especially, the silence itself is part of the rite's teaching.

participationGuides.title: How to Participate at a Sung Mass
participationGuides.body: At a Sung Mass, the ceremonial pacing is slower and the chant often carries the liturgical action. Let the chant teach you where you are. Watch the altar, listen for major sung Ordinary parts, and use the guide to rejoin at the next stable landmark instead of chasing every line in real time. The goal is reverent orientation, not constant synchronized reading, and not treating the schola like a stopwatch.

participationGuides.title: When You Are Not Receiving Communion
participationGuides.body: Remain prayerful and recollected. Many visitors stay kneeling, follow the Communion prayers in the guide, and make a spiritual communion while the faithful receive sacramentally. You do not need to imitate external actions that do not belong to your state in order to participate fruitfully. The rite still invites thanksgiving, humility, and desire for union with Christ.

chantGuides.title: What Gregorian Chant Is
chantGuides.summary: Gregorian chant is the Church's traditional sung prayer: free in rhythm, text-led, and ordered toward worship rather than performance.
chantGuides.body: Gregorian chant is meant to clothe the sacred text, not distract from it. Even when you do not know every chant, it can still help you pray by slowing your attention and keeping the liturgy's pace reverent. In this app, the chant notes are intentionally small and practical: enough to orient you, not to turn the app into a chant encyclopedia or a substitute for the rite itself.

chantGuides.title: Where Chant Appears in the Mass
chantGuides.summary: In Sung Masses, chant often marks the entrance, Ordinary parts such as Kyrie and Sanctus, the proper chants, and other major transitions.
chantGuides.body: If you are new, do not worry about naming every chant. Use it as a landmark. The entrance, Gradual or Alleluia, Offertory, Sanctus, Agnus Dei, and Communion chant often tell you where the liturgy is moving even before you find the line on the page. The app’s Sung Mass profile leans on these landmarks more heavily than the Low Mass profile does.

chantGuides.title: How to Listen and Follow
chantGuides.summary: At a Sung Mass, you can participate fruitfully by listening, watching the altar, and rejoining at stable textual landmarks rather than forcing constant page-turning.
chantGuides.body: The schola and ministers may move at a different pace from a hand missal. That is normal. Use the guide to hold the larger structure of the rite in mind, and let chant carry the atmosphere of prayer without anxiety about matching every syllable in real time. If you drift, wait for the next strong landmark instead of trying to recover every lost line. The point is attentive worship, not perfect synchronization.
